description: Improvements and enhancements to the bd-view TUI application based on usage feedback and identified gaps.
title: Beads TUI Viewer Improvements

description:
Replace the parenthesized text status indicators in the tree panel with compact Unicode symbols for improved scannability and reduced visual clutter. The current indicators (( ), (~), (!), (z), (x)) should become single-character symbols that are more visually distinct.

## Context

The tree panel currently uses text-based status indicators like `( )` for open, `(~)` for in_progress, etc. These occupy 3 characters each and are visually similar. Single Unicode symbols would be more compact, easier to scan, and more visually distinctive.

## Design

- Modify `statusIcon()` in `internal/ui/model.go` to return Unicode symbols instead of parenthesized text
- Proposed symbol mapping:
  - `open` → `○` (white circle)
  - `in_progress` → `◉` (filled circle) or `▶` (play)
  - `blocked` → `✗` (cross mark)
  - `deferred` → `◌` (dotted circle)
  - `closed` → `✓` (check mark)
- Keep existing color assignments for each status
- Update `renderTreeRow()` formatting if column widths change
- Update golden test files to reflect new symbols
- Ensure symbols render correctly in common terminal emulators (xterm, alacritty, kitty, Windows Terminal)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `statusIcon()` returns a single Unicode symbol per status instead of parenthesized text
- [ ] Each status has a distinct, recognizable symbol
- [ ] Existing status colors (yellow for in_progress, red for blocked, green for closed, faint for deferred) are preserved
- [ ] Tree rows are visually more compact and easier to scan
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated to match new symbols
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated and match the new output
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-01: Use symbols for task status indicators in tree panel

description:
Display a truncated bead title after the ID, type, and status on each tree row when the panel is wide enough, so users can identify beads without selecting them for the detail pane.

## Context

Tree rows currently show only `<id>  <type>  <status>` (e.g., `bd-view-0ny.3  task  ○`). When a project has many beads, IDs alone are not enough to distinguish them — users must arrow through each one and read the detail pane. Showing even a partial title on the row (e.g., `bd-view-0ny.3  task  ○  Match theme colors...`) dramatically improves scannability.

## Design

- Modify `renderTreeRow()` in `internal/ui/model.go` to append the bead title after the status icon
- Calculate available width for the title:
  - `availableWidth = treePanelWidth - prefixWidth - expandIndicatorWidth - idWidth - typeWidth - statusWidth - padding`
  - Only show the title if `availableWidth` >= some minimum (e.g., 10 characters)
- Truncate the title with an ellipsis (`…`) if it exceeds available width
- Style the title in a dimmed/faint style so it doesn't compete with the ID and status for visual attention
- The tree panel width needs to be accessible in `renderTreeRow()` — pass it as a parameter or store it in the model
- Handle edge cases:
  - Very narrow panels: hide the title entirely, show only ID/type/status as today
  - Titles shorter than available width: show in full, no ellipsis
  - Empty titles: show nothing extra
- Update golden test files to include title previews

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tree rows display a truncated bead title after the status icon when width permits
- [ ] Title is truncated with `…` when it exceeds the available space
- [ ] Title is styled in faint/dimmed to visually separate it from ID, type, and status
- [ ] Title is hidden entirely when the tree panel is too narrow (graceful degradation)
- [ ] Full titles shorter than available space are shown without ellipsis
- [ ] Beads with empty titles show no extra content on the row
- [ ] Tree row alignment is consistent across rows with varying title lengths
- [ ] Selected row highlighting still applies correctly to the full row including title
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated and match the new output
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-10: Show truncated title preview on tree rows

description:
Save the expand/collapse state of tree nodes to a local file so that when bd-view is restarted, the tree opens with the same nodes expanded as when the user last exited.

## Context

The tree currently starts with all nodes either expanded or collapsed based on the `--expand-all` flag. Every time the user restarts bd-view, they must manually re-expand the nodes they care about. For projects with many epics and nested tasks, this is tedious. Persisting the expand/collapse state provides continuity between sessions.

## Design

- Store expand state in a JSON file at `.beads/bd-view-state.json` (co-located with the beads database):
  ```json
  {
    "expanded_nodes": ["bd-view-0ny", "bd-view-sz3"]
  }
  ```
- On startup, after building the tree via `BuildTree()`:
  - Load the state file if it exists
  - Set `Expanded = true` only for nodes whose IDs appear in `expanded_nodes`
  - If the state file doesn't exist or is unreadable, fall back to the `--expand-all` flag behavior
  - The `--expand-all` flag should override persisted state when explicitly passed
- On every expand/collapse toggle (`ToggleExpand`, `ExpandAll`, `CollapseAll`):
  - Collect all currently expanded node IDs
  - Write them to the state file asynchronously (fire-and-forget, don't block the UI)
- On tree refresh (polling update):
  - Preserve expanded state of existing nodes
  - New nodes default to collapsed (unless `--expand-all` is active)
  - This is partially handled already — verify and document the behavior
- Handle edge cases:
  - State file contains IDs that no longer exist in the database (ignore them silently)
  - State file is corrupted or invalid JSON (log warning, fall back to defaults)
  - Permission errors writing state file (log warning, continue without persistence)
  - Concurrent bd-view instances (last-write-wins is acceptable)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Expand/collapse state is saved to `.beads/bd-view-state.json` on every toggle
- [ ] On startup, previously expanded nodes are restored from the state file
- [ ] Missing or unreadable state file falls back to `--expand-all` flag behavior
- [ ] `--expand-all` flag overrides persisted state when explicitly passed
- [ ] `ExpandAll` and `CollapseAll` operations update the persisted state
- [ ] Stale node IDs in the state file are silently ignored
- [ ] Corrupted state file logs a warning and falls back to defaults
- [ ] Write errors do not crash or block the UI
- [ ] Tree refresh preserves expand state of existing nodes
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-11: Persist tree expand/collapse state across restarts

description:
Refactor the help overlay to use a data-driven keybinding registry so that all keybindings are defined in one place and the help overlay is generated automatically, ensuring it always shows a complete and accurate reference.

## Context

The current help overlay (`renderHelpOverlay()` in `internal/ui/model.go`, line ~1190) is a hard-coded list of `fmt.Sprintf` calls. Each time a new keybinding is added (e.g., `y` for yank, mouse click for focus), the developer must remember to update the help overlay separately. This is error-prone — keybindings can easily become undocumented. A data-driven approach defines bindings once and generates both the key handling and the help text from the same source.

## Design

- Define a keybinding registry as a structured slice or map, e.g.:
  ```go
  type Keybinding struct {
      Keys        []string // display strings: "j / Down", "Tab", "y"
      Description string   // "Move selection down", "Copy bead ID"
      Section     string   // "NAVIGATION", "TREE", "SEARCH & FILTER", "OTHER"
  }
  ```
- Populate the registry with all current and new keybindings in a single location (e.g., `keybindings.go` in `internal/ui/`)
- Refactor `renderHelpOverlay()` to iterate over the registry, grouping by section and rendering each entry
- Align key labels to a consistent column width for readability (auto-calculate from the widest key string)
- Optionally: use the registry to also drive `Update()` key dispatch (more ambitious, can be a follow-up)
- Ensure the help overlay scrolls if there are more bindings than fit on screen (currently it does not scroll)
- Add scroll support: `j/k` or `Up/Down` to scroll within the help overlay when it overflows

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All keybindings are defined in a single registry data structure
- [ ] `renderHelpOverlay()` is generated from the registry, not hard-coded
- [ ] Adding a new keybinding to the registry automatically appears in the help overlay
- [ ] Keybindings are grouped by section (Navigation, Tree, Search & Filter, Other)
- [ ] Key labels are aligned to a consistent column for readability
- [ ] Help overlay scrolls when content exceeds the available height
- [ ] Existing help overlay visual appearance is preserved or improved
- [ ] `?` toggle and `Esc` to close continue to work
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated if help overlay output changed
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-12: Dynamic help overlay with complete keybinding reference

description:
Allow users to click on a specific row in the tree panel to select that bead and display its details in the detail pane, providing a natural mouse-driven browsing experience.

## Context

US-06 adds mouse click support for shifting focus between panels, but does not handle clicking on a specific tree row to select it. Users expect that clicking a bead in the tree will highlight that row and load its detail — this is standard behavior in any tree-based UI. This task builds on the mouse infrastructure from US-06 to add row-level click targeting.

## Design

- Requires mouse support to be enabled (depends on US-06 or can be done independently by adding `tea.WithMouseCellMotion()`)
- Handle `tea.MouseMsg` with left click in the `Update()` function in `internal/ui/model.go`:
  - If click x-coordinate is within the tree panel region:
    - Calculate the clicked row index from `msg.Y`:
      - Subtract header offset (1 line for "Beads" header)
      - Add `m.treeScroll` to account for scroll position
      - Result is the index into the visible nodes list
    - If the calculated index is valid (within bounds of visible nodes), set `m.selectedIdx` to that index
    - Trigger detail pane refresh for the newly selected bead (same as keyboard navigation does)
    - Shift focus to tree pane if not already focused
- Handle edge cases:
  - Click on the header row ("Beads"): ignore, don't change selection
  - Click below the last tree row: ignore, don't change selection
  - Click while search or filter overlay is active: ignore tree clicks
  - Double-click on a parent node: could toggle expand/collapse (optional enhancement)
- In narrow/overlay mode: clicking should be handled appropriately (tree takes full width)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Left-clicking a tree row selects that bead and updates the detail pane
- [ ] Clicked row becomes highlighted as the selected row
- [ ] Click correctly accounts for tree scroll offset
- [ ] Click on the header row is ignored
- [ ] Click below the last visible row is ignored
- [ ] Click during search input or filter overlay is ignored
- [ ] Focus shifts to tree pane on tree row click
- [ ] Detail pane loads the clicked bead's full information
- [ ] Works correctly in narrow/overlay mode
- [ ] Mouse click selection works alongside keyboard navigation without conflicts
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-13: Click tree row to select bead and show its detail

description:
Highlight occurrences of the active search query within tree rows and the detail pane so users can immediately see why a bead matched and where the term appears.

## Context

When using `/` search, the tree is filtered to show only matching beads and their ancestors, but there is no visual indication of *where* the query matched within each row. Users must scan the ID and title themselves to spot the match. Highlighting the matched substring (e.g., with a background color or bold+color) is standard behavior in search UIs and makes results much easier to parse. If US-02 (full-field search) is implemented, highlighting becomes even more important in the detail pane to show matches within description, design, or acceptance criteria.

## Design

- Create a helper function `highlightMatches(text string, query string, style lipgloss.Style) string` that:
  - Finds all case-insensitive occurrences of `query` in `text`
  - Wraps each occurrence with the highlight style (e.g., bold + bright yellow background)
  - Preserves the original casing of the matched text
  - Returns the styled string
- Apply highlighting in `renderTreeRow()` in `internal/ui/model.go`:
  - Highlight matches in the bead ID and title portions of the row
  - Only apply when `m.searchQuery != ""`
  - Ensure highlighting composes correctly with the selected row's reverse style
- Apply highlighting in `renderDetailPanel()`:
  - Highlight matches in the title, description, design, acceptance criteria, and notes sections
  - Only apply when `m.searchQuery != ""`
  - Ensure highlighting works within glamour-rendered markdown (if US-07 is done) or the current `renderMarkdown()` output
- Handle edge cases:
  - Query matches across a style boundary (e.g., part bold, part normal): highlight the visible portions
  - Empty search query: no highlighting applied
  - Special regex characters in query: treat query as literal string, not regex
  - Very long highlight spans: no special handling needed, style the full match

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Matched substrings in tree row IDs are highlighted with a distinct style
- [ ] Matched substrings in tree row titles are highlighted (if US-10 title preview is present)
- [ ] Matched substrings in detail pane content are highlighted
- [ ] Highlighting is case-insensitive but preserves original casing
- [ ] Highlight style is visually distinct (e.g., bold with background color)
- [ ] Highlighting composes correctly with selected row reverse style
- [ ] No highlighting is applied when search query is empty
- [ ] Special characters in search query are treated as literals
- [ ] Highlighting is removed when search is cleared (Esc)
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-14: Highlight search query matches in tree and detail pane

description:
Enable mouse wheel scrolling in both the tree panel and detail panel, with the scroll target determined by the mouse cursor position.

## Context

Currently, scrolling the tree and detail pane is only possible via keyboard (`j/k` or `Up/Down`). Users with a mouse expect to scroll whichever panel the cursor is hovering over. With mouse support enabled (from US-06), Bubble Tea delivers `tea.MouseMsg` events for scroll wheel actions that include cursor coordinates, making position-aware scrolling straightforward.

## Design

- Requires mouse support to be enabled via `tea.WithMouseCellMotion()` (from US-06, or added independently)
- Handle `tea.MouseMsg` with scroll wheel actions in `Update()` in `internal/ui/model.go`:
  - `tea.MouseActionScrollUp` and `tea.MouseActionScrollDown` (or `tea.MouseWheelUp` / `tea.MouseWheelDown` depending on Bubble Tea version)
  - Determine which panel the cursor is over by comparing `msg.X` against the tree panel width (same hit-testing as US-06/US-13)
- If cursor is over the **tree panel**:
  - Scroll up: decrement `m.treeScroll` (clamped to 0)
  - Scroll down: increment `m.treeScroll` (clamped to `len(visibleNodes) - viewportHeight`)
  - Do not change `m.selectedIdx` — scroll is independent of selection
- If cursor is over the **detail panel**:
  - Scroll up: decrement `m.detailScroll` (clamped to 0)
  - Scroll down: increment `m.detailScroll` (clamped to max content lines)
- Scroll step: 3 lines per scroll event (common default, could be configurable later)
- Handle edge cases:
  - Scroll when help overlay or filter overlay is active: scroll the overlay content if it supports scrolling (US-12), otherwise ignore
  - Scroll in narrow/overlay mode: scroll whichever view is currently displayed
  - No content to scroll (tree fits in viewport): ignore scroll events gracefully
  - Focus should NOT shift on scroll — only Tab/click changes focus

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Mouse wheel scroll up/down works in the tree panel when cursor hovers over it
- [ ] Mouse wheel scroll up/down works in the detail panel when cursor hovers over it
- [ ] Scroll target is determined by cursor x-position relative to the panel split
- [ ] Tree scroll is clamped within valid bounds (0 to max)
- [ ] Detail scroll is clamped within valid bounds (0 to max)
- [ ] Scrolling does not change the selected bead or panel focus
- [ ] Scroll step is a reasonable default (e.g., 3 lines per event)
- [ ] Scroll works correctly in narrow/overlay mode
- [ ] Scroll events during help/filter overlay are handled gracefully
- [ ] Mouse scroll does not interfere with keyboard scrolling
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-15: Mouse wheel scroll for tree and detail panels

description:
Sort beads within each tree level by dependency order so that independent (unblocked) tasks appear before tasks that depend on them, giving users a natural top-down execution order.

## Context

The tree currently sorts nodes by priority (ascending) then ID (alphabetical) within each parent. This means a task that blocks three others may appear after them if its ID sorts later. Dependency-aware sorting would surface the critical path — independent/blocking tasks first, then the tasks they unblock — making the tree a natural task execution plan.

## Design

- Modify the sort logic in `BuildTree()` in `internal/tree/tree.go` to use topological ordering within each parent's children:
  1. Build a local dependency graph among siblings (children of the same parent) using `blocks` dependencies
  2. Topological sort: nodes with no incoming `blocks` edges (independent tasks) come first
  3. Break ties using the existing sort criteria (priority ascending, then ID alphabetical)
  4. If no dependency relationships exist among siblings, the sort is identical to today
- Cross-parent dependencies (a task under one epic blocking a task under another) should not affect sort — only same-parent siblings are reordered
- Handle cycles gracefully: if a cycle exists among siblings, fall back to the existing sort for those nodes and optionally log a warning
- The dependency data is available in `Bead.Dependencies` (array of `RelatedBead` with `DependencyType`)
- Consider adding a `--sort` CLI flag to choose between `dependency` (new default) and `priority` (current behavior) if the change is controversial

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Independent (unblocked) tasks appear before tasks that depend on them within each parent
- [ ] Blocking tasks appear before the tasks they block
- [ ] Ties among independent tasks are broken by priority then ID (existing behavior)
- [ ] Cross-parent dependencies do not affect sibling sort order
- [ ] Cycles among siblings fall back to priority+ID sort gracefully
- [ ] Sort order updates correctly on tree refresh when dependencies change
- [ ] Existing tree navigation and selection preservation work correctly with new sort
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] New unit tests cover: independent-first ordering, chain ordering, cycle fallback, mixed deps/no-deps
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-16: Sort tree nodes by dependency order

description:
Allow users to select text in the detail panel using click-and-drag and automatically copy it to the clipboard on mouse release, enabling easy extraction of content like URLs, code snippets, or acceptance criteria from bead details.

## Context

With mouse support enabled (from US-06), normal terminal text selection is captured by the TUI. Since bd-view uses AltScreen mode, the terminal does not provide native text selection — the application must handle it.

Right-click cannot be used as a copy trigger because Windows Terminal (and other terminals) intercepts right-click for paste/context menu before it reaches the application, even when mouse capture is active. This is a terminal emulator limitation, not a Bubble Tea issue. Copy-on-release using only left-click events is the most portable approach.

Clipboard write uses Bubble Tea v2's native `tea.SetClipboard()` (from US-19 upgrade), which emits OSC 52 escape sequences through the program's output channel.

## Design

### Text Selection and Copy

- **Left click-and-drag → select and copy:**
  - Track mouse press (`tea.MouseActionPress` with left button) in the detail panel to record selection start (row/col)
  - Track mouse motion (`tea.MouseActionMotion` with left button held) to update selection end and render highlight in real-time
  - Highlight selected text with a distinct style (e.g., reverse video or background color) during rendering
  - Selection should work across multiple lines
  - On `tea.MouseActionRelease`, if selection spans more than zero characters:
    - Copy the selected text to clipboard via `tea.SetClipboard(selectedText)`
    - Show a brief confirmation in the status bar (e.g., "Copied 3 lines")
    - Keep the selection highlight visible after copy
  - A simple click (press + release without drag) does NOT copy — it only clears any existing selection and sets focus

- **Clear selection:**
  - Single left click (no drag), any keypress, or scroll clears the selection and highlight

### Coordinate-to-Text Mapping

- Store the rendered detail content lines in the model (as `[]string`) after each render
- On mouse events, map screen coordinates to content:
  1. Subtract detail panel origin (x, y) from mouse position
  2. Add `m.detailScroll` to the y coordinate to get the content line index
  3. Use the x coordinate as the column offset within that line
  4. Strip ANSI escape sequences for content extraction (keep styled text for display)
- For multi-line selection, extract all lines between start and end positions

### Why Not Right-Click

Windows Terminal intercepts right-click for paste/context menu before it reaches the application, even with mouse capture active. This is a terminal emulator behavior, not a Bubble Tea limitation. Other terminals (iTerm2, Kitty, Alacritty) do pass right-click through, but targeting only left-click ensures portability across all terminals.

- Help overlay should document: "Click and drag in detail panel to select and copy text"

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Click-and-drag in the detail panel creates a visible text selection
- [ ] Selected text is highlighted with a distinct visual style (reverse video or background color)
- [ ] Selection spans multiple lines correctly
- [ ] Selected text is automatically copied to clipboard on mouse release via `tea.SetClipboard()`
- [ ] Simple click (no drag) does NOT trigger copy
- [ ] Status bar shows confirmation on successful copy
- [ ] Selection highlight remains visible after copy until explicitly cleared
- [ ] Selection clears on next left click, keypress, or scroll
- [ ] Selection correctly accounts for detail panel scroll offset and x/y position
- [ ] Works on all terminals supporting OSC 52 (Windows Terminal, iTerm2, Kitty, Alacritty)
- [ ] Help overlay documents click-and-drag copy workflow
- [ ] Drag starting in the tree panel does not trigger detail selection
- [ ] Mouse selection does not interfere with tree row click (US-13) or panel focus (US-06)
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-17: Text selection and copy in detail panel

description:
Preserve the detail panel scroll position when the underlying bead content is refreshed via polling, but reset it to the top when the user navigates to a different bead.

## Context

The detail panel currently refreshes its content when the polling cycle detects changes in the selected bead's data. If the user has scrolled down to read acceptance criteria or notes, a refresh snaps them back to the top, losing their place. Conversely, when switching to a different bead, the scroll position from the previous bead may carry over, showing the middle of the new bead instead of the top. Both behaviors are disorienting.

## Design

- **On polling refresh** (same bead selected, content updated):
  - Preserve `m.detailScroll` at its current value
  - Clamp to new content length if the updated content is shorter than before (so scroll doesn't go past the end)
  - This applies to both `m.detailScroll` (detail panel) and `m.treeScroll` (tree panel)
- **On bead selection change** (user navigates to a different bead via keyboard or mouse):
  - Reset `m.detailScroll` to 0
  - Tree scroll should NOT reset — keep the tree viewport stable
- Identify where selection changes happen in `Update()`:
  - `j/k`/`Up/Down` in tree pane → changes `m.selectedIdx` → reset detail scroll
  - Mouse click on tree row (US-13) → changes `m.selectedIdx` → reset detail scroll
  - Search filtering that changes visible nodes → preserve selection if same bead, reset if different
- Identify where refresh happens:
  - `tickMsg` handler that re-fetches data → preserve scroll if selected bead ID hasn't changed
- Store `m.lastSelectedBeadID` to detect whether the selected bead actually changed between updates

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Detail panel scroll position is preserved when content refreshes for the same bead
- [ ] Detail panel scroll resets to 0 when navigating to a different bead
- [ ] Tree panel scroll position is preserved on content refresh
- [ ] Scroll is clamped to valid bounds if refreshed content is shorter
- [ ] Keyboard navigation (j/k) to a new bead resets detail scroll
- [ ] Mouse click on a different tree row resets detail scroll
- [ ] Search/filter that keeps the same bead selected preserves detail scroll
- [ ] Search/filter that changes the selected bead resets detail scroll
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-18: Preserve scroll position on refresh, reset on bead switch

description:
Upgrade from Bubble Tea v1 to v2 to gain native clipboard support (`tea.SetClipboard()`), improved mouse handling, and other framework improvements.

## Context

bd-view currently uses Bubble Tea v1 (v1.3.10) which has no clipboard support. Multiple improvement tasks depend on clipboard functionality (US-09: yank bead ID, US-17: text selection and copy). Bubble Tea v2 provides native `tea.SetClipboard(string)` and `tea.ReadClipboard()` via OSC 52, plus improved mouse event handling that will benefit US-06, US-13, US-15, and US-17. This upgrade is a prerequisite for reliable clipboard and improved mouse features.

## Design

- Update `go.mod` to use `charm.land/bubbletea/v2` (note: v2 moved to `charm.land` from `github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea`)
- Update all imports from `github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea` to `charm.land/bubbletea/v2`
- Address breaking changes in v2:
  - Review the v2 migration guide / changelog for API changes
  - `tea.Msg` types may have changed (especially `tea.KeyMsg`, `tea.MouseMsg`)
  - `tea.WindowSizeMsg` → check for renamed fields
  - Program initialization and options may have changed
  - `tea.WithMouseCellMotion()` → verify equivalent in v2
- Update related Charm dependencies as needed:
  - `github.com/charmbracelet/lipgloss` → check compatibility with bubbletea v2
  - `github.com/charmbracelet/x/exp/teatest` → check if test framework migrated
  - `github.com/charmbracelet/x/exp/golden` → verify compatibility
- Fix all compilation errors after dependency update
- Run `go vet` and `go test ./...` to verify nothing is broken
- Regenerate golden test files if output changed
- Verify the TUI launches and behaves identically to v1 (navigation, search, filter, resize, refresh)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `go.mod` references `charm.land/bubbletea/v2`
- [ ] All imports updated throughout the codebase
- [ ] `tea.SetClipboard()` is available for use by US-09 and US-17
- [ ] All existing functionality works identically (tree navigation, detail pane, search, filter, help overlay, polling refresh)
- [ ] Mouse support via v2 equivalent of `tea.WithMouseCellMotion()` works
- [ ] `tea.MouseMsg` events are correctly handled with v2 API
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated for v2 API changes
- [ ] Integration tests pass with v2 teatest framework
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated if output changed
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
- [ ] `go build ./...` compiles cleanly
title: US-19: Upgrade to Bubble Tea v2

description:
Expand the `/` search to match against all bead fields — description, acceptance criteria, design, and notes — in addition to the current ID and title matching. This enables users to find beads by any content they remember.

## Context

The current `filterBySearch()` in `internal/ui/model.go` only matches against `node.Bead.ID` and `node.Bead.Title`. Users often remember keywords from a bead's description or acceptance criteria but cannot locate the bead without browsing manually. Full-text search across all fields makes the search feature significantly more useful.

## Design

- Modify `filterBySearch()` in `internal/ui/model.go` to also match against:
  - `node.Bead.Description`
  - `node.Bead.AcceptanceCriteria`
  - `node.Bead.Design`
  - `node.Bead.Notes`
- The detail data for each bead must be available at search time. Currently, detail is fetched lazily via `bd show` only for the selected bead. Options:
  - **Option A**: Pre-fetch all bead details on initial load and refresh (may be expensive for large databases)
  - **Option B**: Search only against data already available in the list response, extend `bd list` fields if needed
  - **Option C**: Perform search server-side via a `bd search` command if available
- Update the help overlay text from "Search by ID or title" to "Search by ID, title, or content"
- Preserve existing ancestor-inclusion logic so parent nodes of matches remain visible in the tree
- Search should remain case-insensitive

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Typing a query in `/` search mode matches against bead description, design, acceptance criteria, and notes fields
- [ ] ID and title matching continues to work as before
- [ ] Search is case-insensitive across all fields
- [ ] Ancestor nodes of matching beads remain visible to preserve hierarchy context
- [ ] Help overlay text updated to reflect expanded search scope
- [ ] Status bar search hint updated if applicable
- [ ] Performance remains acceptable with databases of 100+ beads
- [ ] All existing search-related unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated if help overlay text changed
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-02: Extend search to match across all bead fields

description:
Align bd-view's color scheme with ralph-tui's theme for a consistent visual experience across tools. Users switching between ralph-tui and bd-view should see a cohesive palette for status colors, borders, highlights, and text styles.

## Context

bd-view currently uses ad-hoc ANSI color codes (e.g., color 226 for yellow, 196 for red, 34 for green) chosen during initial development. ralph-tui has an established color theme that users are familiar with. Aligning the two tools reduces cognitive load when switching between them.

## Design

- Audit ralph-tui's color definitions to identify the exact palette:
  - Status colors (open, in_progress, blocked, deferred, closed)
  - Border/frame colors (active vs inactive panels)
  - Highlight/selection colors
  - Header and heading styles
  - Faint/dimmed text styles
- Create a centralized `theme.go` or `styles.go` file in `internal/ui/` to define all color constants in one place, replacing scattered inline `lipgloss.Color()` calls
- Map ralph-tui's color values to lipgloss styles
- Update all rendering functions in `model.go` to reference the centralized theme
- Ensure `--no-color` flag continues to disable all color output

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All status colors match ralph-tui's palette (open, in_progress, blocked, deferred, closed)
- [ ] Border/frame colors match ralph-tui's theme for active and inactive panels
- [ ] Selection highlight color matches ralph-tui
- [ ] Header and section heading styles are consistent with ralph-tui
- [ ] Color definitions are centralized in a single theme file (not scattered across rendering code)
- [ ] `--no-color` flag still disables all colors correctly
- [ ] Visual appearance is cohesive when running bd-view and ralph-tui side by side
- [ ] All existing unit and golden tests pass or are updated
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated to match new colors
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-03: Match theme colors to ralph-tui

description:
Replace the ASCII tree-drawing characters in the tree panel with Unicode box-drawing symbols for a cleaner, more polished appearance. The current connectors (|, ├──, └──) should become proper Unicode equivalents.

## Context

The tree panel currently uses a mix of ASCII and Unicode characters for hierarchy connectors. The vertical bars use `│` (Unicode) but some spacing and alignment could be improved. More importantly, the expand/collapse indicators use ASCII brackets `[-]` and `[+]` which look dated alongside Unicode box-drawing characters. Adopting consistent Unicode symbols throughout would give the tree a more modern, cohesive look.

## Design

- Modify `renderTreeRow()` in `internal/ui/model.go` to use refined Unicode symbols:
  - Expand/collapse indicators: `[-]` → `▼` (expanded), `[+]` → `▶` (collapsed)
  - Verify existing connectors (`├── `, `└── `, `│   `) are consistent Unicode box-drawing characters
- Adjust spacing/padding in `renderTreeRow()` to account for character width differences between ASCII brackets and Unicode triangles
- Update `fmt.Sprintf` format string in `renderTreeRow()` to accommodate new indicator widths
- Update golden test files to reflect new symbols
- Ensure symbols render correctly in common terminal emulators (xterm, alacritty, kitty, Windows Terminal)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Expanded parent nodes show `▼` instead of `[-]`
- [ ] Collapsed parent nodes show `▶` instead of `[+]`
- [ ] All tree connectors use consistent Unicode box-drawing characters (`├── `, `└── `, `│   `)
- [ ] Leaf nodes (no children) have no expand/collapse indicator
- [ ] Tree alignment is consistent across all depth levels
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated to match new symbols
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated and match the new output
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-04: Use symbols for tree sub-item connectors

description:
Enhance the visual feedback when switching focus between the tree panel and detail panel so users always have a clear indication of which panel is active and will receive keyboard input.

## Context

Currently, focus is indicated only by a colored header ("Beads" turns color 86 when the tree panel is focused) and the selected row uses `Reverse(true)`. The detail panel has no visible focus indicator at all. When tabbing between panels, the visual change is subtle and easy to miss, especially on monitors with limited color range or in bright environments.

## Design

- Add a visible border color change on the focused panel:
  - Active panel: highlighted border color (e.g., bright cyan or matching ralph-tui's active border)
  - Inactive panel: dimmed/default border color
- Add a focus indicator to the detail panel header (currently has no focus styling)
- Consider adding a subtle background tint or bold border on the focused panel
- Modify `renderTreePanel()` in `internal/ui/model.go`:
  - Change `BorderStyle` and/or border color based on `m.focusedPane == treePane`
- Modify `renderDetailPanel()` in `internal/ui/model.go`:
  - Add border or header styling based on `m.focusedPane == detailPane`
- Ensure the selection highlight in the tree panel remains visible regardless of focus state
- Update golden test files to reflect new focus styling

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The focused panel has a visually distinct border color or style
- [ ] The unfocused panel has a dimmed or default border
- [ ] Detail panel header shows a focus indicator when detail pane is focused
- [ ] Tree panel header continues to show a focus indicator when tree pane is focused
- [ ] Focus change via Tab is immediately visually apparent
- [ ] Selected row in tree panel remains visible when focus moves to detail panel
- [ ] Focus indicators work correctly in narrow/overlay mode
- [ ] `--no-color` flag disables focus color indicators gracefully
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated and match the new output
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-05: Improve focus visual feedback between panels

description:
Enable users to shift focus between the tree panel and detail panel by clicking with the mouse, in addition to the existing Tab key binding.

## Context

Bubble Tea supports mouse input via `tea.WithMouseCellMotion()` which delivers `tea.MouseMsg` events with screen coordinates and button information. Currently bd-view does not enable mouse support at all. Adding mouse click to shift focus is a natural and low-effort interaction that complements keyboard navigation, especially for users who are browsing rather than power-navigating.

## Design

- Enable mouse support by adding `tea.WithMouseCellMotion()` to the `tea.NewProgram()` options in `cmd/bd-view/main.go`
- Handle `tea.MouseMsg` in the `Update()` function in `internal/ui/model.go`:
  - On left click (`tea.MouseLeft` with `tea.MouseActionPress`), determine which panel was clicked by comparing `msg.X` against the tree panel width (the split point)
  - If click is in the tree panel region → set `m.focusedPane = treePane`
  - If click is in the detail panel region → set `m.focusedPane = detailPane`
- The split point is calculated during `renderView()` — store the tree panel width in the model so it's available during `Update()`
- Optionally: clicking a specific row in the tree panel could also select that bead (calculate row index from `msg.Y` minus header offset plus `m.treeScroll`)
- Mouse support should not interfere with terminal copy/paste (cell motion mode preserves this)
- In narrow/overlay mode, clicks should be handled appropriately (no panel split exists)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Mouse support is enabled via `tea.WithMouseCellMotion()`
- [ ] Left-clicking in the tree panel region shifts focus to the tree panel
- [ ] Left-clicking in the detail panel region shifts focus to the detail panel
- [ ] Focus change via mouse click triggers the same visual feedback as Tab (border color, header highlight)
- [ ] Mouse click does not break existing keyboard navigation
- [ ] Terminal copy/paste still works (cell motion mode, not all motion)
- [ ] Mouse interaction is handled gracefully in narrow/overlay mode
- [ ] Tree panel width is tracked in the model for hit-testing
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-06: Shift panel focus using mouse click

description:
Replace the hand-rolled `renderMarkdown()` function with the glamour library to render description, design, acceptance criteria, and notes as properly formatted terminal markdown with headings, bold, italic, lists, code blocks, and tables.

## Context

The current `renderMarkdown()` in `internal/ui/model.go` (line ~1099) is a minimal custom implementation that only handles code block fencing (with faint styling), inline bold (`**text**`), and line wrapping. It does not render headings, italic, bullet lists, numbered lists, tables, links, or nested formatting. Since bead content is written in markdown, users see raw syntax (e.g., `- [ ] criteria`) instead of formatted output. The glamour library from Charm (same team as Bubble Tea/Lip Gloss) provides full markdown rendering for terminals with configurable styles.

## Design

- Add `github.com/charmbracelet/glamour` as a dependency
- Create a glamour `TermRenderer` configured with:
  - Word wrap set to the detail pane's content width
  - A style that complements the existing bd-view theme (dark style as baseline, customized to match ralph-tui colors if US-03 is done)
  - No extra trailing newlines
- Replace the body of `renderMarkdown(text string, width int) string` to call the glamour renderer instead of the custom line-by-line logic
- Remove the now-unused `wrapLine()` and `renderInlineBold()` helper functions
- Handle edge cases:
  - Empty or whitespace-only input (return empty string, no glamour call)
  - Very narrow widths (glamour has a minimum usable width)
  - `--no-color` flag: use glamour's `NoTTY` style or `ascii` style to strip ANSI
- The section headings (DESCRIPTION, DESIGN, etc.) are rendered separately with lipgloss and should remain unchanged — only the section content goes through glamour
- Update golden test files to reflect glamour's formatted output

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `glamour` dependency is added to `go.mod`
- [ ] `renderMarkdown()` uses glamour for terminal markdown rendering
- [ ] Headings (`#`, `##`) in bead content render with proper styling
- [ ] Bold and italic text render correctly
- [ ] Bullet lists and numbered lists render with proper indentation
- [ ] Checkbox lists (`- [ ]`, `- [x]`) render recognizably
- [ ] Code blocks render with distinct background or faint styling
- [ ] Inline code renders with distinct styling
- [ ] Tables render in a readable format
- [ ] Content wraps correctly to the detail pane width
- [ ] `--no-color` flag produces readable unformatted output
- [ ] Empty sections continue to be hidden (no empty glamour output)
- [ ] Unused `wrapLine()` and `renderInlineBold()` functions are removed
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated to match glamour output
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-07: Render markdown in detail pane using glamour

description:
Display a progress fraction (e.g., `3/5`) next to epic nodes in the tree panel showing how many child beads are closed out of the total, giving an at-a-glance sense of completion.

## Context

Epic nodes in the tree currently show the same information as any other bead: ID, type label, and status icon. Since epics are containers for work, users frequently want to know how much of an epic is done without expanding it and counting manually. A compact progress indicator next to the epic row makes this instantly visible.

## Design

- Modify `renderTreeRow()` in `internal/ui/model.go` to append a progress indicator for nodes with `IssueType == "epic"` (or more broadly, any node with children)
- Calculate progress from the node's `Children` slice:
  - Count children where `Bead.Status == "closed"` → `done`
  - Total children count → `total`
  - Display as `[done/total]` e.g., `[3/5]`
- Style the indicator:
  - All done (`done == total`): green/success color
  - Partially done: default or yellow
  - None done (`done == 0`): faint/dimmed
- Position: after the status icon, at the end of the row
- Only show for nodes that have children (don't show `[0/0]` for leaf nodes)
- Consider recursive counting (children of children) vs direct children only — start with direct children for simplicity
- Update golden test files to include progress indicators

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Epic nodes in the tree show a `[done/total]` progress fraction
- [ ] Progress counts direct children with `closed` status as done
- [ ] Fully complete epics show the fraction in green/success color
- [ ] Partially complete epics show the fraction in default or yellow
- [ ] Epics with no closed children show the fraction in faint/dimmed style
- [ ] Leaf nodes (no children) do not show a progress indicator
- [ ] Progress updates correctly when the tree refreshes with new data
- [ ] Collapsed epics still show the progress indicator
- [ ] Tree row alignment remains consistent with the added indicator
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass or are updated
- [ ] Golden test files are regenerated and match the new output
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-08: Show progress indicator for epics in tree panel

description:
Allow users to press `y` to copy the currently selected bead's ID to the system clipboard for quick pasting into other terminals or tools.

## Context

Users frequently need to reference bead IDs when working with the `bd` CLI in another terminal (e.g., `bd show bd-view-0ny.3`, `bd update bd-view-0ny.3 --claim`). Currently they must manually read and type the ID from the tree panel. A yank-to-clipboard shortcut eliminates this friction, especially for IDs with complex prefixes.

## Design

- Handle the `y` key in the `Update()` function in `internal/ui/model.go` when focused on the tree pane
- Get the selected bead's ID from the current visible nodes list using `m.selectedIdx`
- Copy to clipboard using Bubble Tea v2's native `tea.SetClipboard(id)` (available after US-19 upgrade), which uses OSC 52 under the hood
- Show a brief confirmation in the status bar (e.g., "Copied: bd-view-0ny.3") that clears after a short duration or on next keypress
- The `y` key should only work when not in search input mode or filter overlay
- Update the help overlay to document the `y` keybinding under the navigation section

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pressing `y` in tree pane copies the selected bead's ID to the system clipboard
- [ ] Uses `tea.SetClipboard()` from Bubble Tea v2
- [ ] Status bar shows a brief confirmation message with the copied ID
- [ ] Confirmation message clears on next keypress or after a timeout
- [ ] `y` key is ignored during search input mode and filter overlay
- [ ] `y` key is ignored when detail pane is focused (or copies the displayed bead ID — decide which is more intuitive)
- [ ] Help overlay lists the `y` keybinding with description "Copy bead ID to clipboard"
- [ ] Works on terminals supporting OSC 52 (Windows Terminal, iTerm2, Kitty, Alacritty)
- [ ] Graceful fallback if clipboard is unavailable — show error in status bar, don't crash
- [ ] All existing unit tests pass
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes
title: US-09: Copy bead ID to clipboard with y key

description:
A terminal user interface (TUI) application for browsing a .beads database in real time. Built with Go + Bubble Tea. The viewer presents a navigable tree of beads on the left panel with type/status indicators, and a scrollable detail pane on the right showing all fields of the selected bead. The display refreshes automatically as the underlying database changes.

See beads-tui-viewer.md for full PRD.
title: Beads TUI Viewer

description:
Set up the Go project with Bubble Tea framework and command-line option parsing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Go module initialized (go.mod) with Bubble Tea, Lip Gloss, and cobra/pflag dependencies
- [ ] Main entry point parses CLI flags: --db <path>, --refresh <seconds> (default 2), --expand-all, --no-color
- [ ] Basic Bubble Tea program launches showing an empty split-panel layout (left tree area, right detail area)
- [ ] Press q to quit cleanly
- [ ] Status bar at bottom shows keybinding hints: [q] Quit [/] Search [f] Filter [?] Help
- [ ] go build produces a working binary
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-01: Project scaffolding with CLI flags and basic Bubble Tea app

description:
Implement the search feature that filters the tree to matching beads.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pressing / opens a search input prompt at the bottom of the screen
- [ ] Typing filters the tree to beads whose ID or title contains the search string (case-insensitive)
- [ ] Matching beads and their ancestors remain visible (to preserve hierarchy context)
- [ ] Escape clears the search and restores the full tree
- [ ] Active search query shown in the status bar
- [ ] Empty search results show a "No matching beads" message
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-10: Search beads by ID or title

description:
Implement the filter menu for filtering beads by type and status.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pressing f opens a filter overlay/menu
- [ ] Filter options: by type (multi-select from available types), by status (multi-select from available statuses)
- [ ] Multiple selections within a category use OR logic (e.g., show tasks OR bugs)
- [ ] Filters across categories use AND logic (e.g., show open tasks)
- [ ] Filtered tree shows matching beads and their ancestors (to preserve hierarchy)
- [ ] Active filter shown in status bar (e.g., "Filter: type=task,bug status=open")
- [ ] Escape clears all active filters
- [ ] --type and --status CLI flags apply initial filters on launch
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-11: Filter beads by type and status

description:
Add a help overlay showing all keybindings and polish the status bar.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pressing ? shows a help overlay listing all keybindings in a readable table format
- [ ] Escape or ? again closes the help overlay
- [ ] Status bar shows: keybinding hints, time since last refresh, active filter/search info
- [ ] All keybindings from the PRD are functional: j/k, Up/Down, Enter, Left/Right, Tab, /, f, Esc, r, e, c, g, G, q, ?
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-12: Help overlay and status bar polish

description:
Set up the test infrastructure: mock bd CLI, golden file snapshots, and teatest integration tests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Mock CommandExecutor that returns fixture JSON data (no real .beads database needed)
- [ ] Golden file infrastructure: testdata/ directory with .golden files, -update flag to regenerate
- [ ] View snapshot tests: tree rendering with correct indentation/icons, detail pane layout, empty states
- [ ] Integration tests with teatest: startup flow shows tree, navigation updates detail, expand/collapse works
- [ ] All tests pass without requiring a real terminal (TTY) or database
- [ ] go test ./... passes cleanly
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-13: Testing infrastructure with mocks and golden files

description:
Implement the data fetching layer that calls bd CLI as a subprocess and parses JSON output into Go structs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Define Go structs matching the bead JSON schema (id, title, description, design, acceptance_criteria, notes, status, priority, issue_type, owner, parent, dependencies, labels, created_at, updated_at, closed_at, close_reason)
- [ ] Implement a CommandExecutor interface for running bd commands (enables test mocking)
- [ ] Function to run bd list --all --json --limit 0 and parse result into []Bead
- [ ] Function to run bd show <id> --json and parse result into Bead
- [ ] --db flag is passed through to bd commands when specified
- [ ] Unit tests with mock executor covering: successful parse, empty list, malformed JSON error handling
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-02: Data layer to fetch and parse beads from bd CLI

description:
Build the in-memory tree structure from the flat bead list, with expand/collapse state tracking.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TreeModel struct that holds root nodes and maps bead IDs to tree nodes
- [ ] Build tree from flat []Bead: group by parent field, top-level beads (no parent) are root nodes
- [ ] Children sorted by priority (ascending) then ID (alphabetical)
- [ ] Each node tracks expand/collapse state (default: collapsed, or expanded if --expand-all)
- [ ] Orphaned children (parent ID not found) appear as root nodes
- [ ] FlattenVisible() method returns ordered slice of visible nodes (respecting collapse state) for rendering
- [ ] Unit tests covering: tree construction, sort order, expand/collapse toggling, orphan handling, flatten with mixed expanded/collapsed
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-03: Build tree model from flat bead list

description:
Render the left panel tree view with proper indentation, tree-drawing characters, and colored status indicators.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each row shows: <id>  <type>  <status> with tree-drawing characters for hierarchy
- [ ] Tree-drawing characters: |-- for middle children, +-- for last child, indentation for depth
- [ ] Type uses short labels: epic, feat, task, bug, chore, adr
- [ ] Status uses color + icon: open=white ( ), in_progress=yellow (~), blocked=red (!), deferred=gray (z), closed=green (x)
- [ ] Currently selected bead row is highlighted
- [ ] Collapsible parent nodes show [-] when expanded, [+] when collapsed
- [ ] Tree panel has a "Beads" header
- [ ] --no-color flag disables all color output
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-04: Render tree panel with type/status indicators

description:
Implement keyboard navigation for the tree panel: movement, expand/collapse, and scroll.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] j/Down moves selection down through visible nodes
- [ ] k/Up moves selection up through visible nodes
- [ ] Enter or Right on a parent node expands it (shows children)
- [ ] Left on an expanded parent collapses it (hides children)
- [ ] Left on a child node moves selection to its parent
- [ ] g goes to top of tree, G goes to bottom
- [ ] e expands all nodes, c collapses all nodes
- [ ] Selection wraps or stops at boundaries (does not crash)
- [ ] Tree scrolls to keep selected item visible in the viewport
- [ ] Unit tests for navigation: up/down movement, expand/collapse, boundary behavior
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-05: Tree navigation with keyboard controls

description:
Implement the right panel detail view showing the selected bead header info and metadata.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Header shows bead ID as the pane title
- [ ] Title field displayed prominently
- [ ] Metadata row: Type, Status (with color), Priority, Owner
- [ ] Parent bead ID shown (if present)
- [ ] Date fields: Created, Updated, Closed (only non-empty dates shown)
- [ ] Horizontal separator between header and body sections
- [ ] Empty state shown when no bead is selected ("Select a bead to view details")
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-06: Detail pane with bead header and metadata fields

description:
Add body sections to the detail pane: description, design, acceptance criteria, notes, and dependencies. Render markdown formatting.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each non-empty section displayed with a heading: DESCRIPTION, DESIGN, ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA, NOTES, DEPENDENCIES
- [ ] Empty sections are omitted entirely (no empty headings)
- [ ] Dependencies section shows both "depends on" and "depended on by" relationships
- [ ] Basic markdown rendering: bold text, bullet lists, code blocks with terminal styling (using glamour or lipgloss)
- [ ] Long content wraps properly within the pane width
- [ ] Detail pane is independently scrollable when focused (Tab to switch focus, j/k to scroll)
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-07: Detail pane body sections with markdown rendering

description:
Wire up the split-panel layout and handle terminal resize, including narrow-mode behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tree panel on left, detail panel on right, status bar at bottom
- [ ] Layout adjusts proportionally on terminal resize
- [ ] Tab key switches focus between tree and detail pane (visual indicator of which pane is focused)
- [ ] On narrow terminals (< 100 cols), detail pane is hidden; Enter on a bead shows detail in full-screen overlay
- [ ] Escape closes the full-screen overlay in narrow mode
- [ ] Minimum terminal size: 80x24 (show message if smaller)
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-08: Split layout with responsive terminal resize

description:
Implement automatic periodic refresh that polls bd list, diffs against in-memory state, and updates the UI without losing user state.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TUI polls bd list --all --json --limit 0 at configurable interval (default 2s from --refresh flag)
- [ ] Diff logic detects added, removed, and updated beads (by comparing IDs + updated_at timestamps)
- [ ] Tree updates in place: added beads appear, removed beads disappear, updated beads refresh
- [ ] User scroll position and selection are preserved after refresh
- [ ] If currently-selected bead was updated, detail pane refreshes automatically
- [ ] If currently-selected bead was deleted, selection moves to nearest neighbor
- [ ] r key forces an immediate refresh
- [ ] Status bar shows "Refreshed Xs ago" with time since last successful refresh
- [ ] Unit tests for diff logic: added/removed/updated detection, selection preservation
- [ ] go vet passes with no issues
title: US-09: Real-time refresh with polling and diff

description: Prepare and publish the first public release of bd-view to GitHub. Covers repository creation, documentation, build automation, and release artifacts so users can discover, install, and run bd-view from a single GitHub release page.
title: Release bd-view v1.0.0 to GitHub

description:
Create the GitHub repository for bd-view and push the existing codebase as the initial commit history.

## Context

The project currently exists only as a local Git repository with no remote. The Go module path is already set to `github.com/timoch/bd-view` in `go.mod`, so the repo name must match.

## Design

- Create the repository on GitHub via `gh repo create timoch/bd-view`:
  - Public repository
  - Description: "A terminal UI for browsing .beads databases in real time"
  - No template, no wiki, issues enabled
- Add the remote: `git remote add origin git@github.com:timoch/bd-view.git`
- Push the existing main branch: `git push -u origin main`
- Verify the module path `github.com/timoch/bd-view` resolves correctly after push
- Add appropriate `.gitignore` if not already present (Go binary, `.beads/dolt/` data, editor files)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] GitHub repository `timoch/bd-view` exists and is public
- [ ] All existing commits are pushed to `origin/main`
- [ ] Repository description is set
- [ ] `.gitignore` covers build artifacts, `.beads/dolt/` data directory, and editor files
- [ ] `go mod download` works when cloning from GitHub
title: US-01: Create GitHub repository and push code

description:
Write a README.md that introduces bd-view, shows what it looks like, explains how to install and run it, and documents key features and keybindings.

## Context

The project has no README. A good README is the first thing users see on GitHub and determines whether they try the tool. It should be concise but complete enough to get someone from discovery to running bd-view in under a minute.

## Design

- Use **vhs** (https://github.com/charmbracelet/vhs) to create a demo GIF:
  - Write a `.tape` script (e.g. `demo.tape`) that launches bd-view with a sample database, navigates the tree, opens a detail panel, and shows key features
  - Generate a GIF from the tape: `vhs demo.tape`
  - Embed the resulting GIF in the README
  - Commit both the `.tape` script and the generated GIF so the demo is reproducible
- Structure:
  1. **Title and tagline** — "bd-view: A terminal UI for browsing .beads databases"
  2. **Demo GIF** — recorded with vhs showing the split-panel view with tree and detail
  3. **Features** — bullet list of key capabilities (real-time refresh, tree navigation, search, filter, vim keybindings, read-only)
  4. **Installation** — document all install methods (see US-06)
  5. **Usage** — basic invocation with flags (`--db`, `--refresh`, `--expand-all`, `--no-color`)
  6. **Keybindings** — table of all keyboard shortcuts
  7. **Requirements** — Go version, `bd` CLI dependency, supported terminals
  8. **License** — reference the LICENSE file
- Keep it under 200 lines — link to the PRD or wiki for deep-dive details
- Use GitHub-flavored markdown with proper headings for navigation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] README.md exists at the repository root
- [ ] Project name and one-line description are at the top
- [ ] A `demo.tape` vhs script exists that demonstrates bd-view in action
- [ ] A demo GIF is generated from the tape and embedded in the README
- [ ] Features section lists key capabilities
- [ ] Installation section documents how to install (binary download, `go install`, build from source)
- [ ] Usage section shows basic command-line invocation with flag descriptions
- [ ] Keybindings section has a complete table of all shortcuts
- [ ] Requirements section lists prerequisites (`bd` CLI, Go version, terminal)
- [ ] License is referenced
- [ ] Markdown renders correctly on GitHub
title: US-02: Add README with project introduction

description:
Add an MIT LICENSE file to the repository to clarify the terms under which bd-view can be used and distributed.

## Context

Open-source projects without a license are legally ambiguous — users cannot safely use or contribute to them. MIT was chosen as it matches the license of all key dependencies (Bubble Tea, Lip Gloss, cobra) and is the simplest, most permissive option.

## Design

- Create a `LICENSE` file at the repository root with the full MIT license text
- Set the copyright to "2026 TiMoch (Julien De Groote)"
- Reference the license in the README (US-02) as "MIT License"

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `LICENSE` file exists at the repository root with full MIT license text
- [ ] Copyright line reads "Copyright (c) 2026 TiMoch (Julien De Groote)"
- [ ] GitHub detects and displays "MIT License" on the repository page
title: US-03: Add LICENSE file

description:
Create build automation for local development (Makefile) and cross-platform release builds (GoReleaser) to produce reproducible binaries for Linux and macOS.

## Context

The project currently builds with a bare `go build` command. For a release, we need cross-compiled binaries for Linux (amd64, arm64) and macOS (amd64, arm64). Windows is not supported — bd-view depends on the `bd` CLI and Dolt which are untested on Windows. GoReleaser is the standard tool for Go release automation and integrates with GitHub releases.

## Design

- **Makefile** for local development:
  - `make build` — build for the current platform to `./bd-view`
  - `make test` — run `go test ./...`
  - `make vet` — run `go vet ./...`
  - `make lint` — run `golangci-lint` if available
  - `make clean` — remove build artifacts
  - `make install` — install binary to `~/.local/bin/` by default (override with `PREFIX=~/.local make install`). Create the target directory if it doesn't exist.
  - Embed version info via `-ldflags` using git tag/commit
- **GoReleaser** (`.goreleaser.yml`):
  - Build targets: linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64
  - No Windows targets
  - Binary name: `bd-view`
  - Entry point: `./cmd/bd-view`
  - Archive format: `.tar.gz`
  - Include LICENSE and README in archives
  - Embed version via ldflags: `-X main.version={{.Version}} -X main.commit={{.Commit}}`
  - Checksum file generation
- Add `version` flag to the CLI (`bd-view --version`) that prints the embedded version
- Add version variable in `cmd/bd-view/main.go` with ldflags placeholder

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `Makefile` exists with build, test, vet, clean, and install targets
- [ ] `make install` defaults to `~/.local/bin/` (no sudo required)
- [ ] Install prefix is configurable via `PREFIX` variable (e.g. `PREFIX=/usr/local make install`)
- [ ] `make install` creates the target bin directory if it doesn't exist
- [ ] `.goreleaser.yml` exists with Linux and macOS build configuration (no Windows)
- [ ] `make build` produces a working binary with embedded version info
- [ ] `make test` runs all tests successfully
- [ ] `goreleaser check` validates the config (dry run)
- [ ] `goreleaser release --snapshot --clean` produces archives for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64
- [ ] `bd-view --version` prints the version, commit, and build date
- [ ] Build artifacts are listed in `.gitignore`
- [ ] `go vet` passes with no issues
title: US-04: Set up build scripts with Makefile and GoReleaser

description:
Tag the release commit, run GoReleaser to build cross-platform binaries, and publish a GitHub release with artifacts, changelog, and checksums.

## Context

With the repository pushed (US-01), build scripts ready (US-04), and documentation in place (US-02, US-03), the final step is to cut the actual release. This produces downloadable binaries and a release page that users can find and install from.

## Design

- Ensure all quality gates pass before tagging:
  - `make test` — all tests pass
  - `make vet` — no issues
  - `go build ./...` — compiles cleanly
- Create an annotated git tag: `git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Initial release of bd-view"`
- Push the tag: `git push origin v1.0.0`
- Run GoReleaser to build and publish:
  - `goreleaser release --clean`
  - This creates the GitHub release, uploads archives for all platforms, generates checksums, and creates a changelog from commit history
- Verify the release:
  - GitHub release page shows all platform archives
  - Checksums file is attached
  - Changelog is populated
  - Download a binary and verify it runs with `--version`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Git tag `v1.0.0` exists and is pushed to origin
- [ ] GitHub release `v1.0.0` is published
- [ ] Release includes archives for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64
- [ ] No Windows archives are included
- [ ] Checksums file is attached to the release
- [ ] Changelog is generated from commit history
- [ ] Downloaded binary runs and prints correct version with `--version`
- [ ] Release page has a clear title and description
title: US-05: Tag release and create GitHub release with artifacts

description:
Document all available installation methods in the README so users can choose the approach that fits their environment.

## Context

After the release is published (US-05), there are multiple ways to install bd-view. Each method should be documented with exact commands so users can copy-paste and go.

## Design

- Document the following installation methods in the README installation section:

1. **Download pre-built binary** (simplest):
   ```bash
   # Download the latest release for your platform
   curl -Lo bd-view.tar.gz https://github.com/timoch/bd-view/releases/latest/download/bd-view_Linux_x86_64.tar.gz
   tar xzf bd-view.tar.gz
   mv bd-view ~/.local/bin/
   ```
   - Default install target is `~/.local/bin/` (no sudo required, follows XDG conventions)
   - Mention `sudo mv bd-view /usr/local/bin/` as an alternative for system-wide install
   - Include platform-specific URLs for Linux amd64/arm64 and macOS amd64/arm64
   - Mention the checksums file for verification

2. **Go install** (for Go developers):
   ```bash
   go install github.com/timoch/bd-view/cmd/bd-view@latest
   ```

3. **Build from source**:
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/timoch/bd-view.git
   cd bd-view
   make build
   # or: go build -o bd-view ./cmd/bd-view
   ```

- `make install` should default to `~/.local/bin/` as the install prefix
- Note the prerequisite: `bd` CLI must be installed and available in PATH
- Note minimum terminal requirements (Unicode support, 256-color)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pre-built binary download is documented with `~/.local/bin/` as the default target
- [ ] System-wide `/usr/local/bin/` install is documented as an alternative
- [ ] Checksum verification steps are included
- [ ] `go install` one-liner is documented
- [ ] Build from source steps are documented
- [ ] `make install` defaults to `~/.local/bin/` prefix
- [ ] `bd` CLI prerequisite is clearly stated
- [ ] Terminal requirements are noted
- [ ] All commands are copy-pasteable and correct
- [ ] Installation section is concise and scannable
title: US-06: Document installation methods in README

description:
Write a vhs tape script and generate a demo GIF showing bd-view in action, for embedding in the README.

## Context

The README (US-02) needs a demo GIF to show users what bd-view looks like. vhs (charmbracelet/vhs) records terminal sessions from `.tape` scripts into GIFs deterministically.

## Design

- Create a `demo.tape` vhs script at the repository root that:
  - Sets appropriate terminal dimensions (e.g. 120x35)
  - Launches bd-view against a sample/demo .beads database
  - Navigates the tree (expand/collapse nodes, move between issues)
  - Opens the detail panel to show issue content
  - Demonstrates search or filter if time permits
  - Keeps the recording short (~10-15 seconds)
- Generate the GIF: `vhs demo.tape` → outputs `demo.gif`
- Optimize GIF size if needed (keep under ~2MB for GitHub)
- Commit both `demo.tape` and `demo.gif`
- The README (US-02) will reference the GIF via `![demo](demo.gif)`

## Prerequisites

- vhs installed (`go install github.com/charmbracelet/vhs@latest`)
- ffmpeg installed
- A sample .beads database with enough issues to look interesting

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `demo.tape` exists at the repository root
- [ ] Running `vhs demo.tape` produces a `demo.gif`
- [ ] GIF shows bd-view launching, navigating the tree, and viewing issue details
- [ ] GIF is under 2MB
- [ ] Both `demo.tape` and `demo.gif` are committed
title: Create demo GIF with vhs for README
